items.instruction: Choose the best answer.
items.question_text: Which airborne military IFR change must still be forwarded to flight service?

choices.text: A change in destination or a change in fuel exhaustion time.
A request for a smoother altitude only.
A routine request for another altimeter setting.
A report of light chop with no flight plan change.

items.explanation: Flight service must receive destination changes and fuel exhaustion time changes from airborne military IFR flights.

items.instruction: Is this an approved traffic-information example?
items.question_text: "Traffic twelve o'clock, seven miles, eastbound, Gulfstream 650, one seven thousand."

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That traffic-information example appears in 5-1-4b.

items.instruction: Which departure action matches the volcanic-ash procedure?
items.question_text: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, which departure handling is allowed?

choices.text: Provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures.
Require every taxiing aircraft to stop for spacing checks.
Suspend all departures until ash is no longer visible.
Route every aircraft back to the ramp for engine inspections.

items.explanation: With volcanic ash on the airport surface, departures are handled as rolling takeoffs.

items.instruction: When is the light-gun warning signal used?
items.question_text: Which situation calls for the alternating red-and-green warning signal?

choices.text: Aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists.
A pilot requests taxi instructions after landing.
A vehicle reports the movement area is clear.
A routine runway assignment is being issued.

items.explanation: Alternating red and green warn when aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists.

items.instruction: When may REIL be turned off?
items.question_text: After REIL has been used with separate controls, which event allows it to be turned off?

choices.text: An arriving aircraft has landed.
The aircraft has reached downwind.
The runway edge lights are set to step 2.
Visibility improves above 5 miles.

items.explanation: One shutdown trigger is that the arriving aircraft has landed.

items.instruction: Which change to normal PAPI operation is allowed?
items.question_text: Which of these may justify varying PAPI operation from the normal table setting?

choices.text: As requested by the pilot.
Whenever RVR is unavailable.
Only after the approach controller approves the setting.
Only when twilight is in progress.

items.explanation: Pilot request is one allowed reason to vary normal PAPI operation.

items.instruction: When may sequenced flashing lights be used?
items.question_text: Which circumstance can justify operating sequenced flashing lights?

choices.text: As requested by the pilot.
Whenever taxiway lights are on.
Only when the ALS is off.
Only after a PAR controller approves the change.

items.explanation: Pilot request is one basis for operating sequenced flashing lights.

items.instruction: What replaces ALSF-2 when the low-visibility trigger is gone?
items.question_text: Once the ALSF-2 low-visibility trigger no longer applies, what should be operated?

choices.text: Operate the SSALR system.
Keep the ALSF-2 system running solely because it was already selected.
Turn off all approach lights until visibility falls below one-half mile.
Leave only the REIL operating.

items.explanation: When that low-visibility trigger is no longer present, SSALR is the system that should be operated.

items.instruction: Which change from the normal HIRL/RCLS/TDZL table is allowed?
items.question_text: Which circumstance allows HIRL, runway centerline lights, or touchdown zone lights to vary from Table 3-4-8?

choices.text: As requested by the pilot.
Only when the approach controller approves the change.
Only when RVR is unavailable.
Whenever the aircraft is within 3 miles of touchdown.

items.explanation: Pilot request is one allowed exception to the normal HIRL, runway centerline light, and touchdown zone light settings.

items.instruction: Which change to normal MIRL operation is allowed?
items.question_text: Which circumstance allows MIRL to vary from the normal table setting?

choices.text: As requested by the pilot.
Only when the tower is closed.
Whenever taxiway lights are on.
Only for military aircraft.

items.explanation: Pilot request is one allowed reason to vary MIRL operation.

items.instruction: Which optional use of high-speed turnoff lights is allowed?
items.question_text: Which circumstance can justify operating high-speed turnoff lights?

choices.text: As requested by the pilot.
Only when taxiway centerline lights are unavailable.
Only between sunset and sunrise.
Only after the aircraft exits the movement area.

items.explanation: Pilot request is one allowed reason to operate high-speed turnoff lights.

items.instruction: Which change to normal taxiway-light operation is allowed?
items.question_text: Which circumstance allows taxiway lights to vary from the normal table settings?

choices.text: As requested by the pilot.
Only when runway edge lights are on step 3.
Only when the airport beacon is on.
Only for taxiing military aircraft.

items.explanation: Pilot request is one allowed reason to vary taxiway-light settings.

items.instruction: Which daytime condition turns the rotating beacon on?
items.question_text: If controls are provided, which daytime condition requires the rotating beacon to operate?

choices.text: The reported ceiling or visibility is below basic VFR minima.
The runway lights are set above step 2.
The pilot requests REIL.
A PAR controller has the ASR out of service.

items.explanation: During daytime, the rotating beacon is turned on when the reported ceiling or visibility is below basic VFR minima.

items.instruction: Which STOL runway action is allowed?
items.question_text: If a pilot asks about a designated STOL runway, what information may be issued?

choices.text: Issue the measured STOL runway length if the pilot requests it.
Assign a designated STOL runway whenever it shortens taxi time.
Assign a STOL runway only to military aircraft.
Issue declared distances instead of measured STOL runway length on pilot request.

items.explanation: If the pilot asks, the measured STOL runway length may be issued.

items.instruction: When should ASDE without safety logic be operating?
items.question_text: Which visibility condition requires operating an ASDE system that does not have safety logic?

choices.text: When visibility is less than the most distant point in the active movement area.
Only when RVR drops below takeoff minimums.
Only when a pilot requests surface radar assistance.
Only when an aircraft is on short final.

items.explanation: ASDE systems without safety logic are operated when visibility is less than the most distant point in the active movement area.

items.instruction: Which taxi-clearance situation must be avoided?
items.question_text: Which condition should a controller avoid creating with a taxi or hover-taxi clearance?

choices.text: Small aircraft or helicopters taxiing in close proximity to taxiing or hover-taxi helicopters.
Small aircraft holding short on a parallel taxiway while a helicopter departs another runway.
Super aircraft taxiing at normal power on an assigned route.
Helicopters taxiing behind fixed-wing aircraft after receiving wake turbulence cautionary advisories.

items.explanation: A clearance should not place small aircraft or helicopters in close proximity to taxiing or hover-taxi helicopters.

items.instruction: Which routing exception is allowed?
items.question_text: Which request allows the normal altitude and distance limitations not to be applied?

choices.text: Requested routing is via an MTR.
The pilot reports the NAVAID received clearly.
The route is on an established airway.
The aircraft is at or above FL 450.

items.explanation: Requested routing via an MTR is one recognized exception to the normal altitude and distance limitations.

items.instruction: When must Mode C be revalidated?
items.question_text: Which event requires fresh validation of the Mode C altitude readout?

choices.text: Track start from coast or frozen status.
Any routine change in beacon code with no other indication of a problem.
Only after the aircraft levels off above FL 180.
Only after the pilot requests altitude verification.

items.explanation: A track that starts from coast or frozen status requires the Mode C altitude readout to be validated again.

items.instruction: Which data block status prevents automated-handoff identification?
items.question_text: Which data block code disqualifies the automated handoff condition from establishing identification?

choices.text: CST.
VFR.
SID.
ALT.

items.explanation: CST is one of the data block indications that prevent automated-handoff identification.

items.instruction: Which aircraft should not get speed adjustment without consent?
items.question_text: Which aircraft may not be assigned speed adjustment unless the pilot consents or another exception applies?

choices.text: An aircraft at or above FL 390 without pilot consent.
An aircraft below FL 390 that is complying with an assigned STAR.
An aircraft on downwind that has not yet reached the final approach fix.
An aircraft being vectored after a published speed restriction is canceled.

items.explanation: Aircraft at or above FL 390 require pilot consent before speed adjustment is assigned.

items.instruction: Which speed assignment is allowed?
items.question_text: Which speed assignment matches the controller's authorized options here?

choices.text: To helicopters, assign a speed not less than 60 knots.
To turbojet departures, assign a speed not more than 210 knots.
To aircraft beneath Class B airspace, assign a speed not less than 200 knots.
To arrival turbojets within 20 flying miles of the runway threshold, assign a speed not less than 230 knots.

items.explanation: One approved assignment is not less than 60 knots for helicopters.

items.instruction: Which departure case is covered here?
items.question_text: Which departure type is addressed by this separation rule?

choices.text: Between aircraft departing from diverging runways.
Between aircraft taxiing to intersecting ramps.
Between missed approaches and localizer back-course entries.
Between crossing runway inspections and departures.

items.explanation: One of the covered cases is aircraft departing from diverging runways.

items.instruction: When may a frequency change be made after the approach has begun?
items.question_text: Which condition allows a post-start frequency change during a single frequency approach?

choices.text: An emergency situation exists.
The aircraft has crossed the final approach fix inbound.
The receiving controller wants an earlier voice check-in.
The pilot has entered IMC after the approach began.

items.explanation: An emergency is one allowed exception for changing frequency after the single frequency approach has begun.

items.instruction: Which terminal automation function belongs here?
items.question_text: Which function is a normal use of terminal automation?

choices.text: Ground speed.
Weather radar depiction.
Runway braking action storage.
Automatic route amendment approval.

items.explanation: Ground speed is one of the normal functions provided by terminal automation.

items.instruction: Which operation is outside approved separation criteria?
items.question_text: Which operation is an example where approved separation criteria do not apply?

choices.text: ADC practice intercept operations.
Standard Class C sequencing to the primary airport.
Normal radar handoff procedures between TRACON sectors.
Routine IFR separation inside published holding patterns.

items.explanation: ADC practice intercept operations are one example where approved separation criteria do not apply.

items.instruction: What must be true if only one missed approach procedure is available?
items.question_text: Which condition must be met before timed approaches can be used when there is only one missed approach procedure?

choices.text: Course reversal is not required.
The missed approach procedure must include a course reversal so spacing can be increased.
Reported ceiling and visibility may be lower than the highest prescribed circling minimums as long as radar vectors are used.
Tower communications may be delayed until after the aircraft passes the final approach fix.

items.explanation: One required condition is that no course reversal be required.

items.instruction: Which wake interval is correct?
items.question_text: Which en route timed-approach wake interval is correct for a small aircraft following a heavy?

choices.text: Small behind heavy - 3 minutes or 6 miles.
Small behind heavy - 2 minutes or 5 miles.
Large behind heavy - 4 minutes or 8 miles.
Category H behind Category B - 2 minutes or 5 miles.

items.explanation: For a small aircraft behind a heavy, the interval is 3 minutes or 6 miles.

items.instruction: Which service is included in Class C service?
items.question_text: Which service is part of the Class C service package?

choices.text: Mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between VFR aircraft.
Automatic vectors to final for every VFR aircraft in the outer area.
Positive separation between all VFR aircraft and terrain.
Holding instructions for every aircraft before entering the Class C area.

items.explanation: Class C service includes mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between VFR aircraft.

items.instruction: Which altitude standard may be used?
items.question_text: When assigning an altitude to a VFR aircraft, which standard may be used?

choices.text: The MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria.
Only hemispheric VFR cruising altitudes with no exception.
Only the altitude requested by the pilot, regardless of terrain or radar minima.
Only the lowest altitude displayed on a terminal automation system.

items.explanation: Assigned VFR altitudes may be based on the MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria.

items.instruction: Which aircraft pair is excepted from this Class C separation requirement?
items.question_text: Which aircraft pairing does not require this Class C separation?

choices.text: Hot air balloons and IFR aircraft.
All VFR fixed-wing aircraft and all IFR aircraft.
Any two helicopters regardless of flight rules or service.
Any VFR aircraft and any military aircraft.

items.explanation: Hot air balloons are one of the aircraft types that need not be separated from IFR aircraft under this Class C exception.

items.instruction: Choose the best answer.
items.question_text: Which oceanic non-RVSM flight is still allowed as an excepted aircraft?

choices.text: An aircraft transporting a spare engine mounted under the wing within the Oakland, Anchorage, or Arctic FIRs.
Any passenger flight that is late and requests a more direct route through RVSM airspace.
Any aircraft operating below FL410 if the pilot reports smooth air.
Any cargo aircraft with one inoperative transponder if it remains in voice contact.

items.explanation: One oceanic exception is an aircraft carrying a spare engine under the wing within the named FIRs, subject to the approval and workload conditions.

items.instruction: Choose the best answer.
items.question_text: Which technique is appropriate when giving radar assistance to a pilot who is not qualified for IFR?

choices.text: Have the pilot lower the gear and slow to approach speed while still in VFR conditions.
Delay all turns until the aircraft is deep in IMC so the pilot can focus on instruments.
Use abrupt maneuvers to get the aircraft out of cloud layers quickly.
Require repeated frequency changes so different sectors can share the workload.

items.explanation: One appropriate technique is to get the aircraft slowed and configured while it is still in VFR conditions.

items.instruction: Which radar-assistance action is appropriate?
items.question_text: When helping a non-IFR-qualified pilot, which action is appropriate?

choices.text: Vector the aircraft to VFR conditions.
Use abrupt maneuvering so the aircraft reaches an airport as quickly as possible.
Delay gear extension until after the aircraft is established in IFR conditions.
Prefer radio frequency changes whenever another controller is available to help.

items.explanation: Vectoring the aircraft toward VFR conditions is one appropriate radar-assistance technique.

items.instruction: Which ELT response action is required?
items.question_text: When an ELT signal is heard or reported, which terminal action should be taken?

choices.text: Attempt to obtain fixes or bearings on the signal.
Wait for the RCC to provide a fix before taking any local action.
Suspend all coordination until the source is confirmed by technical operations.
Leave the position to search the airport if the traffic load permits.

items.explanation: A required local response is to try to obtain fixes or bearings on the ELT signal.
